title: Switzerland’s 10 Million Question
text:
Switzerland’s 10 Million Question

Cedric Gemehl

Gavekal Research | 11 Jun 2026

Consider an economy that over the past 25 years has recorded budget surpluses in 16 years, while reducing its public debt burden from 52% to 39% of GDP. Its industrial sector has consistently accounted for more than 20% of economic output and it has maintained a persistent and expanding trade surplus. Inflation has remained remarkably contained, never exceeding 3.3%, even during the 2022 global inflation shock. Over the same period, its equity market delivered an average annual return of 9.2%, while its currency appreciated by an average of 2.7% a year.In a nutshell, this is the economy Western policymakers have long dreamed of. The irony is that this macroeconomic miracle already exists: it is called Switzerland. Yet few people outside the country could name a single Swiss politician. That is the beauty of the Alpine nation’s system of direct democracy, which gives citizens regular veto and agenda-setting power over public policy through the repeated use of referendums and popular initiatives.
